props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · Research Proposal: Real-Time Brand Intelligence (Madison)

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Marketing teams operate in platform silos, producing inconsistent brand voice across channels. Voice inconsistency measurably hurts business, reducing purchase intent by 23% among multi-platform customers. When model parameters correlate weakly with human judgment, those parameters simply don't predict 'on-brand.' So brand fit is emergent and context-dependent, not something a fixed parameter set can capture.

artifactLines: Marketing teams operate in platform silos, producing inconsistent brand voice across channels.
Voice inconsistency measurably hurts business, reducing purchase intent by 23% among multi-platform customers.
When model parameters correlate weakly with human judgment, those parameters simply don't predict 'on-brand.'
So brand fit is emergent and context-dependent, not something a fixed parameter set can capture.

beats.narration_text: Here is why Research Proposal: Real-Time Brand Intelligence (Madison) belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

artifactLines: Marketing teams operate in platform silos, producing inconsistent brand voice across channels.
Voice inconsistency measurably hurts business, reducing purchase intent by 23% among multi-platform customers.
When model parameters correlate weakly with human judgment, those parameters simply don't predict 'on-brand.'
So brand fit is emergent and context-dependent, not something a fixed parameter set can capture.

beats.narration_text: The finding that model parameters correlate weakly (r below 0.5) with human 'on-brand' judgment fascinates me, because it says brand fit is emergent, not parameterizable. I want to probe it. Help me: (1) design a small study comparing parameter scores against human ratings across platforms, (2) explain what a low correlation rules out versus what it cannot, and (3) suggest one context-dependent dimension we might have failed to measure.

props.command: The finding that model parameters correlate weakly (r below 0.5) with human 'on-brand' judgment fascinates me, because it says brand fit is emergent, not parameterizable. I want to probe it. Help me: (1) design a small study comparing parameter scores against human ratings across platforms, (2) explain what a low correlation rules out versus what it cannot, and (3) suggest one context-dependent dimension we might have failed to measure.
output: They’re not sure if they’re buying from a friend or a corporation
The silent failure: Marketing teams operate in platform silos
